subsystem: The command-line client input line parser of the MySQL/MariaDB `mysql` monitor. `add_line()` is the routine that consumes one line of user-typed SQL/client input, strips or preserves comments, recognizes client-side backslash commands (e.g. \c, \N), splits statements on the current delimiter, tracks string/quote and /* */ comment state, and appends the resulting text to the global query buffer (the `String &buffer`). It rewrites the `line` buffer IN PLACE: a read cursor `pos` scans forward while a write cursor `out` compacts the accepted characters back into the same `line` storage.
provenance: Identified by strong MySQL/MariaDB client idioms: the `DBUG_ENTER("add_line")` / `DBUG_RETURN` tracing macros, the `String` class with `.append()/.length()/.realloc()/.alloced_length()/.c_ptr()/.is_empty()`, the `COMMANDS *com` / `find_command()` / `com->func` / `com->takes_params` client-command dispatch, `charset_info`, `my_isspace`, `my_ismbchar`, `my_strnncoll`, `use_mb`, `HAVE_READLINE`/`add_history`/`not_in_history`, `SERVER_STATUS_NO_BACKSLASH_ESCAPES`, and the global `delimiter`/`delimiter_length`/`preserve_comments`. This is the `add_line` function from client/mysql.cc. The in-code banner `/* sample: primevul_train_2332_fixed */` marks it as the post-fix revision of a PrimeVul pair; the file name suffix `cwe863` is a dataset label and does not describe the actual code, which is reviewed blind.
trust_boundary: The `char *line` argument is fully attacker/user-controlled: it is the raw text the user (or a piped/scripted stdin, or a `SOURCE`d SQL file) typed at the client prompt, delivered via readline. Every byte of `line`, its length, its multi-byte sequences, its quote/backslash/comment structure, and its delimiter placement are adversary-chosen. `*in_string` and `*ml_comment` are caller-persisted parser state carried across lines and are likewise influenced by prior attacker input. The function writes back into that same `line` storage (in place) and into the caller's `String &buffer`. `buff[80]` is a local stack buffer. The security of the whole routine rests on one invariant: the write cursor `out` must never overtake the read cursor `pos`, so that in-place compaction never writes past the storage that backs `line`.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Signature and locals. `buffer` is the accumulating query buffer (an owning String, appended to as statements are recognized). `line` is the single user-typed line to process, editable in place. `in_string` is a by-pointer flag/char holding the currently open quote character (0 when not inside a string) so quote state survives across lines. `ml_comment` is a by-pointer flag for being inside a /* ... */ multi-line comment, also persisted across lines. `truncated` says the input line was cut short by the reader. Locals: `inchar` holds the current scanned byte; `buff[80]` is a fixed 80-byte stack scratch buffer used only for the 'Unknown command' message; `pos` is the forward read cursor and `out` is the in-place write cursor; `com` receives a matched client command; `need_space`/`ss_comment` track pending-space insertion and server-side (/*! */) comment state. `DBUG_ENTER` is debug tracing.

Fast-exits when the line is empty and there is nothing buffered. Optionally records the line in readline history. Then it snapshots the end of the line as `end_of_line = line + strlen(line)` — a pointer to the terminating NUL. This end pointer is captured ONCE, up front, and is later handed to `my_ismbchar` as the multi-byte scan bound. Because the loop rewrites `line` in place, `end_of_line` is a stable reference to the ORIGINAL line extent, not to the compacted contents. The `(uint)` cast of `strlen` truncates the length on a >4GB line, which is not a realistic client input.

The core scan. `pos` and `out` both start at `line`; the loop reads `inchar = *pos` and terminates at the NUL. This is the classic in-place compaction pattern: `out` can only ever lag `pos` because every branch that writes has consumed at least as many bytes as it emits. When comments are not preserved, leading whitespace of a fresh statement (out still at line start, buffer empty) is skipped with `continue`.

Multi-byte handling. `my_ismbchar(charset_info, pos, end_of_line)` returns the byte length of a valid multi-byte character starting at `pos`, bounded by `end_of_line`, or 0. When outside a comment (or preserving comments), all `length` bytes are copied verbatim from `pos` to `out` via `while (length--) *out++ = *pos++;`, then `pos--` compensates for the loop's own `pos++`. Here `out` advances exactly as many bytes as `pos`, so the in-place invariant (out <= pos) is preserved. Inside a discarded comment, the bytes are skipped with `pos += length - 1`. The `end_of_line` bound is what keeps the multi-byte copy from reading past the buffer.

Backslash-command recognition, only when not in a comment and not inside a string with backslash-escapes disabled. `*++pos` consumes the character after the backslash; if that is the terminating NUL, it breaks (a trailing lone backslash). Inside a string, or for the special `\N` NULL token, the backslash is treated as literal data: it emits `\` and normally the following char (two writes for two consumed bytes). The `pos--` case (a backtick that matches the open quote) emits only `\` and rewinds one, again keeping `out` no further than `pos`. This branch is written carefully so consumed-bytes >= emitted-bytes.

If the escaped char names a real client command, the accepted prefix is flushed to `buffer` and `out` is reset to `line`. The command handler `com->func` is invoked with the buffer and `pos-1`. If it takes parameters, the parameter text is consumed up to either the end of a server-side comment (scanning for `*/`) or the current delimiter (scanning with `is_prefix(pos+1, delimiter+1)` so a multi-char delimiter is matched fully). These scans advance `pos` only (they consume input) and clamp at the NUL (`if (!*pos) pos--`), so they never advance `out`. Note the lookahead `*(pos + 1)` inside the `*/` scan reads one byte ahead of `pos`; it is reached only when `*pos != '*'` short-circuits leave it, and `pos` is bounded by the NUL loop condition.

When the backslash char is not a known command, it formats a diagnostic into the fixed `buff[80]` with `sprintf` using a STRING-LITERAL format and a single `%c`. The rendered text is the constant "Unknown command '\X'." — a fixed ~21 bytes regardless of the byte value of `inchar`, so it cannot exceed 80. The format is a literal, not attacker-controlled, so this is not a format-string issue. It then emits the literal `\` and the char (two writes for the two consumed bytes).

Delimiter (statement-terminator) handling, only outside comment and string. `is_prefix(pos, delimiter)` detects the (possibly multi-character) delimiter; `pos += delimiter_length` consumes it. With comment preservation, following whitespace is copied 1:1 (out advances with pos). The accumulated statement is flushed to `buffer`; a trailing single-line comment may be appended. The statement is then dispatched either as a named client command or executed via `com_go`, and the buffer is reset. The lookahead `pos[1]`/`pos[2]` reads are guarded by short-circuit `&&`: `pos[2]` is only read after `*pos=='-'` and `pos[1]=='-'`, so `pos[2]` is at worst the terminating NUL, which is inside the buffer.

Single-line comment detection: a `#`, or a `-- ` sequence (dash-dash followed by whitespace or end-of-line). Again `pos[1]` and `pos[2]` are only dereferenced under short-circuit after confirming earlier bytes are non-NUL, so the reads stay within the NUL-terminated buffer. The accepted prefix is flushed. When preserving comments, the remainder of the line is appended to the buffer; a lone comment line is sent immediately via `com_go`. The branch then `break`s out of the scan (rest of line is comment).

Multi-line comment open/close. `/*` (not `/*!`) opens a comment: with preservation it copies both bytes (two writes, two reads); otherwise it just consumes the `*` (loop consumes the `/`), sets `*ml_comment=1`, and flushes. `*/` closes the comment when not in a server-side (/*!) comment: preservation copies both bytes; otherwise it consumes the `/`, clears `*ml_comment`, and sets `need_space=1`. The author's inline comment states the safety argument explicitly: closing a comment consumes at least 2 bytes but will emit at most 1 extra space later, so `out` still cannot overtake `pos`. Lookahead `*(pos+1)`/`*(pos+2)` are short-circuited behind non-NUL predecessors.

The default 'ordinary character' branch. It tracks server-side executable comments (`/*!` opens, `*/` closes `ss_comment`), and maintains string state: the current char closing an open quote clears `*in_string`, and a quote outside a comment/string opens one. Finally, unless inside a discarded comment, it emits the char. If `need_space` was set by a prior `*/` close and the current char is not whitespace, it first emits a single space, then the char. This is the only place `out` can gain an EXTRA byte relative to consumption, and it is exactly the +1 the earlier comment-close budgeted for by consuming 2 bytes. The whole in-place safety hinges on that accounting.

Line-finalization. `length = out - line` is the number of compacted bytes. Unless truncated or the line begins with the 9-byte token 'delimiter' (a `DELIMITER` command whose continuation must not get a spurious newline), it appends a `'\n'` into `line` (one more in-place write, `*out++`) and bumps `length`. It then ensures the query buffer has room, growing it by `length + IO_SIZE` if needed, and appends the compacted `line` bytes to `buffer`. `buffer.append` returning nonzero (allocation failure) causes a quit return. The final `*out++='\n'` write is only safe if `out` had lagged `pos` throughout the scan, leaving at least one free byte of slack in the `line` storage.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The central, load-bearing contract of the whole routine is that the in-place write cursor `out` NEVER overtakes the read cursor `pos` (equivalently, out-line <= pos-line at all times). `line` is a fixed caller-owned buffer sized to the input; the function writes back into it via the many `*out++ = ...` sites. If any branch ever emits more bytes than it has consumed, `out` runs past the end of `line` and corrupts adjacent memory — an out-of-bounds write / buffer overflow (CWE-787) driven purely by attacker-crafted line content. Every branch below must be audited to prove emitted-bytes <= consumed-bytes; the safety is emergent from arithmetic scattered across ~15 branches, which is exactly why this parser class is historically fragile. In this revision the accounting appears to hold (each write path consumes >= it emits), but the invariant is implicit and unenforced.

This is the single place where `out` can advance by TWO bytes (a synthesized space plus the char) while the current iteration consumed only ONE byte. Its safety depends entirely on the pre-condition that `need_space` was set only by a comment-close (`*/`) branch that consumed 2+ bytes and emitted 0, banking exactly the 1-byte credit spent here (see lines 219-227 and the author's own comment). If any future edit sets `need_space` on a path that did NOT bank that credit, or if two spaces could be synthesized before consuming input, `out` overtakes `pos` and overflows `line` (CWE-787). Confirm that `need_space` is set ONLY where >=2 bytes were consumed with no emission, so the +1 here can never make out exceed pos.

After the scan, a newline is written in place at `out`. This assumes at least one free byte remains in the `line` storage beyond the compacted content. That slack exists only because `out` lagged `pos` during the scan (the input always contained at least the consumed bytes plus its NUL terminator). If the in-place invariant were ever violated so that `out == line + strlen(original_line)` (no slack) or beyond, this append writes past the buffer — a one-byte or worse out-of-bounds write (CWE-787). It is the terminal consumer of the whole in-place-slack contract, so it is the place a subtle over-emit bug would first manifest as memory corruption.

The multi-byte copy trusts `my_ismbchar` to return a `length` that both (a) does not exceed the bytes remaining before `end_of_line`, and (b) is matched by copying exactly that many bytes to `out`. Because `end_of_line` was snapshotted from the ORIGINAL line and `out`/`pos` move together here, the copy is in-place-safe; but the read bound is only correct if `my_ismbchar` honors the `end_of_line` argument for every configured charset. A charset callback that returns a length reaching past `end_of_line` would cause an out-of-bounds READ (CWE-125) of up to `length-1` bytes past the NUL. Confirm the active charset's mb-length function never returns a length that crosses `end_of_line`.

Several branches look one and two bytes ahead of `pos` (`pos[1]`, `pos[2]`, `*(pos+1)`, `*(pos+2)` at lines 130-131, 154, 161-162, 193-194, 210, 231-232, 234). Each relies on C short-circuit `&&`: a deeper index is only read after the shallower one tested non-NUL, so the deepest read lands at worst on the terminating NUL, which is in bounds for a properly NUL-terminated C string. The invariant to uphold is that `line` is ALWAYS NUL-terminated before entry. If a caller ever passed a non-NUL-terminated `line` (or one whose NUL was overwritten in place — it is not here, since out<=pos), these lookaheads become out-of-bounds reads (CWE-125). Low risk given the readline contract, but worth confirming the terminator guarantee.

`sprintf` into the fixed `buff[80]` uses an unbounded primitive. Here it is safe: the format is a string LITERAL (so no CWE-134 format-string exposure despite `inchar` being attacker-influenced — the byte lands in a `%c`, not in the format), and the rendered result is the constant text plus one character, ~21 bytes, far under 80. The only way this becomes a defect is a future edit that widens the message or adds attacker-length `%s` content; snprintf(buff, sizeof(buff), ...) would make the bound explicit. Flagged as a defensive/low item, not an active overflow.

summary: This is the MySQL/MariaDB client `add_line()` in-place SQL line parser. It is the post-fix revision of a PrimeVul pair and, as shown, appears to correctly maintain its core safety invariant — the compaction write cursor `out` never overtakes the read cursor `pos` — so no active memory-corruption bug is demonstrable in the shown code. The real risk is latent and contract-shaped: the absence of an out-of-bounds write (CWE-787) into the caller's `line` buffer depends on emitted-bytes<=consumed-bytes accounting spread across ~15 branches, most delicately the `need_space` +1 emission budgeted by the `*/` comment-close and the terminal `*out++='\n'`. Secondary items are a charset-callback-dependent multi-byte read bound (CWE-125) and an unbounded-but-currently-safe sprintf into buff[80].

reviewed_anchors.anchor: add_line(String &buffer, char *line, char *in_string, bool *ml_comment, bool truncated) signature (lines 2-3)
reviewed_anchors.reason: `line` is fully attacker-controlled and is rewritten in place; the routine's safety is a signature-level contract.
Fixed 80-byte stack buffer written by sprintf; bounded here but flagged as a defensive/low item.
The out<=pos invariant is the central out-of-bounds-write contract of the function.
Only site that emits +1 byte over consumption; safety depends on the */ close having banked the credit.
Snapshot end pointer used only as the my_ismbchar read bound; the (uint) cast only matters for implausible >4GB lines.
Read bound depends on the charset callback honoring end_of_line; possible over-read (CWE-125).
Emits at most the same number of bytes it consumed (2 for 2); preserves out<=pos.
Unbounded primitive; safe today (literal format, fixed ~21-byte output) but a defensive concern.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: delimiter branch pos+=delimiter_length and is_prefix scans (lines 111-114, 90-97)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Advance pos (consume input) only; do not move out, so cannot overflow.
Copies 1:1 with pos already past the delimiter; out stays <= pos.
Short-circuited so worst read is the NUL terminator; latent CWE-125 only if line is not NUL-terminated.
Copies 2 bytes for 2 consumed; the non-preserve path consumes 2 and emits 0 (banks the need_space credit).
Boolean state flags only; no buffer write or arithmetic on their own.
Single-byte state carried by pointer; alters branch selection but writes no buffer.
Terminal in-place write that consumes the slack the whole invariant guarantees; first place an over-emit bug would corrupt memory (CWE-787).
Growth uses length bounded by the line size; String::append checks its own allocation and its failure is handled (DBUG_RETURN(1)).

findings.title: In-place line compaction relies on an implicit out<=pos invariant; any over-emit is an out-of-bounds write into the caller's line buffer
findings.anchor: for (pos=out=line ; (inchar= (uchar) *pos) ; pos++)  with all *out++ = ... write sites
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0122_cwe863.cpp (this function; the buffer belongs to the caller in client/mysql.cc)
findings.invariant: At every write site the number of bytes emitted through `out` since loop start must be <= the number of bytes consumed through `pos`, so that `out` never advances past `pos` and thus never past the end of the `line` storage.
findings.failure_mode: If any branch emits more bytes than it consumed for attacker-crafted input (e.g. a synthesized space or newline added without a matching consumed byte), `out` overtakes `pos` and the `*out++` writes land beyond the end of the caller-owned `line` buffer, corrupting adjacent memory. As shown the accounting holds, so this is a latent contract finding rather than a demonstrated overflow.
findings.what_to_confirm: Prove, branch by branch, that emitted<=consumed holds for every path (especially need_space, the /* */ copies, and the final newline), and that the `line` allocation always has at least one spare byte beyond strlen for the trailing newline append.

findings.title: need_space synthesizes an extra output byte; safety depends on the */ close having consumed >=2 bytes with no emission
findings.invariant: `need_space` may be set true ONLY on a path (the `*/` comment close) that consumed at least 2 input bytes while emitting 0, so the one extra space emitted here is pre-paid and `out` still cannot exceed `pos`.
findings.failure_mode: This is the only site that can push out ahead by an extra byte relative to the current iteration's consumption. If a future edit sets `need_space` where the credit was not banked, or reaches this with need_space set and no consumed slack, `out` overruns `line` (out-of-bounds write). The current code sets need_space only at the `*/` close (line 227), so it is safe today.
findings.what_to_confirm: Grep every assignment of `need_space=1` and verify each is on a path that consumed >=2 bytes and emitted 0 before this site runs.

findings.title: Multi-byte character copy trusts the charset callback's returned length to respect end_of_line
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read (charset-length not bounded by buffer)
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (charset mb-length callback; file not provided)
findings.invariant: `my_ismbchar` must never return a byte length that would read past `end_of_line` for the active `charset_info`, so the copy of `length` bytes from `pos` stays within the original line.
findings.failure_mode: A crafted incomplete multi-byte sequence at the tail of the line, combined with a charset whose mb-length routine returns a length that crosses `end_of_line`, would copy (and read) up to `length-1` bytes past the terminating NUL — an out-of-bounds read; the copied garbage would also propagate into the query buffer. The provided `end_of_line` argument is the intended guard.
findings.what_to_confirm: That every registered charset's my_ismbchar honors the `end` argument and returns 0 (not a positive length) for a truncated sequence at end_of_line.

findings.title: Fixed-size sprintf scratch buffer buff[80] (bounded today, uses an unbounded primitive)
findings.invariant: The formatted output must always fit in `buff[80]`; the format must remain a string literal so attacker bytes only ever land in variadic arguments (`%c`), never in the format itself.
findings.failure_mode: Not exploitable as written: the literal format renders a constant ~21-byte message plus one `%c` byte, well under 80, and because the format is a literal there is no CWE-134 format-string exposure even though `inchar` is attacker-influenced. The concern is purely that sprintf carries no bound, so any later widening of the message (e.g. adding a `%s`) could overflow buff[80]. snprintf(buff, sizeof(buff), ...) would make the bound explicit.
findings.what_to_confirm: That the message stays fixed-length and no `%s`/attacker-length substitution is ever added to this sprintf.

audit_checklist: Prove the out<=pos invariant holds on EVERY branch: for each *out++ site show emitted-bytes since loop start <= consumed-bytes (pos advance).
Confirm need_space=1 is set ONLY on paths that consumed >=2 input bytes with zero emission (the */ comment close), so the +1 at lines 243-246 is always pre-paid.
Verify the caller always passes a NUL-terminated `line` so the pos[1]/pos[2]/*(pos+1)/*(pos+2) lookaheads never read past the terminator.
Confirm the `line` allocation reserves at least one byte of slack beyond strlen so the terminal *out++='\n' (line 268) cannot write out of bounds.
Verify my_ismbchar honors end_of_line for every charset and returns 0 for a truncated multi-byte sequence at the buffer tail (no over-read at lines 34-39).
Confirm the sprintf at line 103 keeps a literal format and fixed-length output; prefer snprintf(buff, sizeof(buff), ...).
Check that com->func callbacks invoked mid-parse (lines 73, 143, 148, 185) do not free or reallocate `buffer`/`line` in a way that invalidates pos/out/end_of_line after return.
Verify buffer.realloc failure (line 272) and buffer.append failure (line 273) are both surfaced; realloc's result is not checked before the subsequent append relies on the grown capacity.
